content.text: Update this branch to the latest remote
I’ll check the current branch, its upstream, and the relevant repo memory first so I update the right remote target without guessing.

Current branch is `main` tracking `origin/main`; the only local change showing is an untracked `.pnpm-store/`. I’m fetching `origin/main` and will fast-forward only, so no merge commit or local rewrite is introduced.

content.text: The branch fast-forwarded cleanly to the remote commit. I’m doing a final status check so I can call out the exact resulting state and any remaining local files.

Updated `main` to `origin/main` with a fast-forward: `c1bbc9c..9947531`.

Current `HEAD` and `origin/main` both point to `99475317089cc75f1451b1893ea31ccd17284a92` (`refactor(token-exchange): remove GitHub App selector`). The only remaining local status item is the pre-existing untracked `.pnpm-store/`. I did not run tests since this was only a branch sync.

Note: SSH fetch failed due missing GitHub SSH auth in this environment, so I fetched the public HTTPS ref and updated `origin/main` from that.

Figure out what’s wrong with the ssh key configuration

I’ll inspect the local SSH and Git configuration first, without reading any private key material. The useful signals are which identity files SSH is trying, whether an agent is present, and whether Git overrides SSH for this repo.

The SSH config is forcing `IdentitiesOnly yes` with a single key path: `~/.ssh/id_ed25519_github`. Agent access was blocked in the sandbox, so I’m going to run the same checks outside the sandbox and then do a verbose GitHub auth probe.

input.justification: Allow checking the macOS SSH agent identities to diagnose why GitHub SSH authentication fails?

content.text: The key file and public key are present, permissioned correctly, and the private/public fingerprints match. The macOS agent has no loaded identities, so the next question is whether SSH can read and offer the configured key directly, and how GitHub responds to it.

input.justification: Allow a verbose SSH authentication probe to GitHub so we can see which key is offered and why it is rejected?

content.text: The verbose probe found the decisive bit: GitHub accepts the configured public key, so the key is authorized on GitHub. The failure happens locally when SSH tries to sign with the private key and macOS Keychain refuses passphrase access in this non-interactive session.

input.justification: Allow checking whether macOS can load SSH identities from Keychain for this non-interactive session?

content.text: There is a second SSH agent socket under `~/.ssh/agent`, but the active `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` points at the empty launchd agent. I’m checking that alternate agent now; if it has the key, this is just an environment socket mismatch.

input.justification: Allow checking the alternate SSH agent socket under ~/.ssh/agent for loaded identities?

result.output:

Process running with session ID 84229
Original token count: 0
Output:

content.text: That alternate-agent check is hanging instead of returning an identity list, which usually means the socket is stale or not responding cleanly. I’m going to poll it once, then stop relying on it if it stays unresponsive.

result.output:

Process running with session ID 84229
Original token count: 0
Output:

content.text: The alternate socket is not responding, so it is not a usable fix path for this session. I’ll interrupt that check and summarize the root cause plus the narrow fix.

result.output:
Chunk ID: 9ada39
Wall time: 0.0008 seconds
Process exited with code 130
Original token count: 0
Output:

content.text:
The SSH key config itself points at the right key, but the runtime cannot use that key non-interactively.

What I found:

- Git remote is SSH: `git@github.com:chikachow/cyspbot.git`
- `~/.ssh/config` forces GitHub to use only `~/.ssh/id_ed25519_github`:
  ```sshconfig
  Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_github
    AddKeysToAgent yes
    UseKeychain yes
    IdentitiesOnly yes
  ```
- The key file exists, permissions are fine, and private/public fingerprints match.
- The active SSH agent has no identities.
- Verbose SSH shows GitHub accepts the public key, then local signing fails:
  ```text
  Server accepts key: /Users/cysp/.ssh/id_ed25519_github ...
  Unexpected keychain error ... User interaction is not allowed.
  No more authentication methods to try.
  git@github.com: Permission denied (publickey).
  ```

So the problem is not that GitHub lacks the key. The problem is that the private key passphrase is not available to this non-interactive session: the key is encrypted, not loaded into the active agent, and macOS Keychain is refusing/passphrase lookup requires user interaction.

The practical fix from an interactive macOS Terminal is:

```sh
ssh-add --apple-use-keychain ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_github
ssh-add -l
ssh -T git@github.com
```

After that, `git fetch origin main` should work from this environment if it gets the same agent/keychain access. The stale-looking socket under `~/.ssh/agent/` also did not respond, so I would not use it as the current `SSH_AUTH_SOCK`.
Can you configure git/ssh to use the gh cli as an auth provider?

I’ll check what `gh` has available locally and whether it is authenticated. If `gh` can provide credentials, the clean route is Git-over-HTTPS with `gh auth git-credential`; SSH itself cannot delegate private-key signing to `gh`.

`gh` has the credential-helper support we need, but its stored GitHub token is currently invalid. I can still wire this repo to use `gh auth git-credential`; actual network operations will succeed only after `gh auth login -h github.com` refreshes the token.
I’ll make the configuration repo-local so it does not change your global Git setup: switch `origin` to HTTPS and add a local credential helper for `https://github.com` backed by `gh`.
I’m writing two local Git config entries now: `origin` becomes HTTPS, and this repo’s GitHub HTTPS credentials are delegated to `gh auth git-credential`.

input.justification: Allow updating this repository's .git/config to use HTTPS origin instead of SSH?
Allow updating this repository's .git/config to use GitHub CLI as the credential helper for github.com?

content.text: Seemed to hang?

The config writes completed; the apparent hang was likely the approval/tool handoff before I could send the final status. I’m verifying the repo-local config now and I’ll also avoid running an authenticated network Git command until the `gh` token is refreshed.

It didn’t leave anything running. The repo-local config changes completed:

```text
origin  https://github.com/chikachow/cyspbot.git
credential.https://github.com.helper !gh auth git-credential
```

So Git is now configured to use HTTPS plus `gh` as the credential helper for this repo.

The remaining blocker is `gh` auth itself:

```text
The token in default is invalid.
To re-authenticate, run: gh auth login -h github.com
```

Once you refresh that login, normal `git fetch`, `git pull`, and `git push` should use `gh` credentials instead of SSH.

content.text:
Fixed.

`gh auth login` completed successfully for `cysp`, with Git protocol set to HTTPS. Verified in the normal unsandboxed context:

```text
✓ Logged in to github.com account cysp (keyring)
Git operations protocol: https
Token scopes: gist, read:org, repo, workflow
```

This repo is also configured to use `gh` for Git credentials:

```text
origin = https://github.com/chikachow/cyspbot.git
credential.https://github.com.helper = !gh auth git-credential
```

I verified `git fetch origin main` now succeeds through that setup. One caveat: sandboxed `gh auth status` still sees the old invalid token/keyring state, so for authenticated `gh` operations from Codex I may need to run them with normal filesystem/keychain access.
Set that credential helper globally
I’ll promote the same GitHub HTTPS credential helper into your global Git config, then remove the repo-local duplicate if it’s now redundant so this checkout does not invoke the same helper twice.

Global Git already has a GitHub helper configured: `!/opt/homebrew/bin/gh auth git-credential`. This is the same helper with an absolute `gh` path, so I’m removing the repo-local override and then verifying the effective config.

input.justification: Allow removing the redundant repo-local GitHub credential helper now that the global helper is already configured?

input.justification: Allow verifying Git fetch still works using the global gh credential helper?

content.text:
Done. The global Git config now supplies the GitHub credential helper:

```text
/Users/cysp/.gitconfig
credential.https://github.com.helper =
credential.https://github.com.helper = !/opt/homebrew/bin/gh auth git-credential
```

The empty helper line is intentional Git config syntax to clear earlier helpers before using `gh`.

I also removed the redundant repo-local helper from this checkout. Verified `git fetch origin main` still works using the global `gh` credential helper.
